steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what the text most strongly suggests about the Martinez-Ramos et al. study. Hartley et al. note that Martinez-Ramos et al. "may have inadvertently excluded studies published before the term "pollinator effectiveness" came into common use" — a methodological limitation that could undermine the study's conclusion.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — The approach selected by the researchers enabled them to sort through and remove obviously incomplete or inconsistent data before conducting their analysis.: frames the exclusion as intentional and beneficial; the passage says it was inadvertent and potentially harmful. ✗
  • Choice B — Due to the difficulty of gathering representative research on angiosperms, the study may have included previous research with a focus too heterogeneous to be comparable.: reverses the problem — the issue is exclusion of relevant studies, not inclusion of incomparable ones. ✗
  • Choice C — Because the study contained data on a limited number of plant species, its conclusion is accurate for those species but cannot be applied to species not in the study.: invents a species-count limitation not mentioned; the passage's concern is about excluded terminology-based studies. ✗
  • Choice D — A limitation in the methodology used by the researchers may entail that the study's conclusion is not well supported.: accurately captures Hartley's implication — inadvertent exclusion is a methodological flaw that weakens the conclusion. ✓
D is correct.
